title: McLachlan-projected reduced dynamics for ill-posed Schrödingerized backward diffusion
abstract: Backward diffusion is a prototype ill-posed evolution: high spatial frequencies grow exponentially in time, so mesh-based time marching without explicit regularization is quickly overwhelmed by noise. Schrödingerization embeds the semidiscrete generator into Hermitian dynamics on an extended space; we ask whether McLachlan projection onto a fixed low-dimensional frame supplies a structured regularizer whose error budget can be read from a projection defect that separates full lifted propagation from the reduced trajectory. We prove uniqueness of the reduced flow, Gram-norm conservation, a lifted--reduced gap bound in terms of that defect, and perturbation estimates that highlight overlap-matrix conditioning when matrix elements are estimated statistically. We also spell out a fair classical baseline -- spectral low-pass or Tikhonov filtering on the same semidiscrete model, with bandwidth or ridge strength matched to the information content of the chosen frame -- so numerical contrasts isolate the Schrödingerized reduced pipeline rather than an unregularized Crank--Nicolson march that mainly showcases blow-up. A calibrated one-dimensional benchmark pairs a spectrally truncated reference with snapshot-built subspace evolution and finite-shot Qiskit Aer estimation, illustrating how lift, projection, and sampling layers contribute differently to the overall error.
summary: The paper demonstrates that projecting Schrödingerized dynamics onto a low-dimensional subspace provides a natural regularization mechanism for ill-posed problems. It proves that this method remains stable even under statistical sampling noise, outperforming unregularized numerical integration.
main_problem: Investigating whether the McLachlan projection of Schrödingerized dynamics can act as a structured regularizer for ill-posed problems like backward diffusion, and quantifying the error budget arising from lifting, projection, and statistical sampling.
main_result: The paper proves that the reduced flow is unique and norm-preserving, and establishes that the projection error serves as a bounded regularizer that prevents the catastrophic blow-up seen in unregularized methods, even when using noisy sampled Hamiltonians.
method: The authors use the Schrödingerization framework to embed non-unitary dynamics into a Hermitian space, then apply McLachlan projection onto a low-dimensional subspace, evaluating the pipeline using Qiskit Aer-simulated Hadamard tests.
model_or_system: A 1D semidiscrete backward diffusion model, which is an ill-posed evolution where high spatial frequencies grow exponentially, embedded into a higher-dimensional Hermitian Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Recovered interior state profiles (u_h), discrete relative L2 error, projection defect (eta_V), and the conditioning of the overlap matrix (S).
important_parameters: Diffusion coefficient (nu = 0.05), final time (T = 0.3), spatial grid size (Nx = 7), and lifted dimension (Np = 16).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the use of a fixed low-dimensional frame for projection and considers the impact of statistical sampling noise on the overlap matrix and Hamiltonian elements.
figures_summary: Figure 2(a) compares recovered interior states across different methods, while Figure 2(b) plots the evolution of the discrete relative L2 error over time for the proposed method, classical baselines, and the unstable Crank-Nicolson method.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the ill-posedness of backward diffusion, defines the Schrödingerization and McLachlan projection framework, provides mathematical proofs for error bounds and stability, and concludes with numerical benchmarks using quantum circuit simulations.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum algorithms, as it provides a rigorous framework for using subspace-based reduced dynamics to stabilize the simulation of non-unitary or dissipative processes on noisy quantum hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Ancillary |0⟩a Hadamard-test schematics for estimating reduced pencil entries after Schrödingerization. (a) Controlled evolution e−iHδ with P ′=I, supplying real and imaginary parts of ⟨Ψ0| e−iHδ |Ψ0⟩for Gram (overlap) reconstruction. (b) Controlled e−iHδ followed by controlled Pauli Pα from H = P

FIG. 2. (a) Recovered interior states uh at T=0.3. (b) Discrete relative L2 error versus the spectral low-pass baseline (∆t=0.03). Crank–Nicolson attains ≈41.99% at the final sample (also the plotted maximum over t). Lifted exact propagation ends at ≈19.37%, classical McLachlan pencils at ≈20.02%, and Aer-sampled pencils at ≈19.82% with a transient peak ≈22.03%, qualitatively consistent with (23).
